label: Video topic
placeholder: Explain what the video will be about.
helper: Describe the subject, event, product, question, story, or idea the video should cover.
why: The script cannot be created without knowing its central subject.

label: Primary goal
placeholder: Select the main goal
helper: Choose the most important outcome the video should achieve.
why: The goal determines the script’s emphasis, structure, evidence, and CTA.

label: Target audience
placeholder: Example: First-time founders building small SaaS products
helper: Describe who should watch and why the topic matters to them.
why: Audience context materially changes vocabulary, examples, pacing, and assumed knowledge.

label: Main viewer promise
placeholder: What should viewers understand, feel, or be able to do afterward?
helper: State the central payoff the video must deliver.
why: A clear promise keeps the hook, body, and conclusion aligned.

label: Essential points and facts
placeholder: List the facts, arguments, steps, names, examples, or details that must appear.
helper: Include only information the final script must not omit.
why: The model must know which user-supplied information is mandatory.

label: Video format
placeholder: Select a format
helper: Choose the format that best matches how the topic should be presented.
why: Different YouTube formats require different narrative and production structures.

label: Output language
placeholder: Example: English, Ukrainian, Russian, Spanish
helper: The complete script and supporting assets will use this language.
why: Language cannot always be safely inferred from the topic or audience.

label: Target duration
helper: Enter the approximate finished video length in minutes, normally between 3 and 60.
why: Duration determines script depth, word budget, number of sections, and pacing.

label: Audience knowledge level
placeholder: Select a knowledge level
helper: Controls how much background explanation and specialist terminology are appropriate.
why: The same subject requires different explanations for beginners and experienced viewers.

label: Tone and style
placeholder: Select a tone
helper: Choose the dominant delivery style without prescribing every sentence.
why: Tone affects word choice, rhythm, emotional intensity, and presenter delivery.

label: Narrator perspective
placeholder: Select a perspective
helper: Choose how the presenter or narrator should address the audience.
why: Perspective influences pronouns, intimacy, authority, and script formatting.

label: Primary call to action
placeholder: Example: Subscribe, comment, download the guide, or visit the product page
helper: Use one primary action. Leave as Auto for a restrained, goal-appropriate CTA.
why: The intended next action changes how the closing and earlier CTA setup are written.

label: Preferred angle
placeholder: Example: Focus on mistakes beginners make rather than a general overview
helper: Specify the viewpoint or framing that should make this video distinctive.
why: A specific angle prevents the script from becoming a generic topic summary.

label: Preferred sections
placeholder: List sections, steps, arguments, or story stages in the preferred order.
helper: Leave blank to let the model build the strongest logical progression.
why: Some creators already have a required sequence or editorial structure.

label: Opening approach
placeholder: Select an opening style
helper: Controls how the script earns attention before providing background.
why: The first moments strongly affect the script’s framing and retention strategy.

label: Narrative structure
placeholder: Select a structure
helper: Choose a progression or let the model select one based on the format.
why: The structure determines how information and payoffs are sequenced.

label: Pacing
placeholder: Select a pacing style
helper: Controls section length, sentence rhythm, and frequency of visual changes.
why: Pacing must match the audience, subject, duration, and editing approach.

label: Retention techniques
placeholder: Select useful techniques
helper: Choose techniques to use naturally, not as artificial engagement tricks.
why: It lets creators influence retention without asking them to design the entire script.

label: Examples or stories
placeholder: Add real examples, personal experiences, scenarios, demonstrations, or analogies.
helper: Only supplied experiences may be presented as real or first-hand.
why: Real examples can make abstract information understandable and credible.

label: Questions to address
placeholder: What doubts, objections, myths, or viewer questions should the script answer?
helper: Use this for important concerns that should be resolved during the video.
why: Addressing genuine viewer uncertainty improves usefulness and narrative progression.

label: Channel context
placeholder: Describe the channel, recurring topics, host, audience relationship, or series context.
helper: Include only context that should influence this specific script.
why: Channel context prevents the script from sounding disconnected from existing content.

label: Voice guidelines
placeholder: Example: Informal, skeptical, dry humor, short sentences, no motivational language
helper: Describe distinctive voice traits or provide a short style sample.
why: A creator’s established voice cannot be reliably inferred from the topic alone.

label: Presenter format
placeholder: Select a presenter format
helper: Determines how spoken sections and production notes are balanced.
why: A face-to-camera script is written differently from a voice-over or screen-recorded tutorial.

label: Available visuals
placeholder: List footage, screenshots, product shots, locations, interviews, charts, or assets available.
helper: The script will not assume access to expensive or unavailable material.
why: Production directions should reflect resources the creator can actually use.

label: Visual-note detail
placeholder: Select the visual-note level
helper: Controls how detailed the B-roll and scene directions should be.
why: Creators have different production workflows and levels of visual planning.

label: On-screen text style
placeholder: Select a text style
helper: Controls callouts, captions, labels, and emphasis text in the production notes.
why: Text-heavy and visually minimal videos need different scripting and editing cues.

label: Editing intensity
placeholder: Select an editing style
helper: Sets the expected frequency of cuts, inserts, visual changes, and pauses.
why: The script’s sentence length and visual rhythm should match the intended editing style.

label: Title direction
placeholder: Select a title style
helper: Controls how the title balances clarity, curiosity, search intent, and viewer benefit.
why: Different channels and topics require different title positioning.

label: Primary search phrase
placeholder: Example: how to validate a SaaS idea
helper: Use only when a specific search phrase matters; natural wording takes priority.
why: A supplied search phrase can align the title, introduction, and description with viewer intent.

label: Supporting search terms
placeholder: Separate a few related phrases with commas.
helper: Use naturally where relevant; never force repetition or keyword stuffing.
why: Related terminology can improve topic coverage when the user has a defined search strategy.

label: YouTube description
placeholder: Choose whether to include it
helper: Auto includes a concise description when it adds practical value.
why: Some users need only the script, while others need a ready-to-upload description.

label: Video chapters
placeholder: Choose whether to include chapters
helper: Auto adds chapters when the duration and structure make them useful.
why: Chapters are useful for structured long videos but unnecessary for every script.

label: Pinned comment
placeholder: Choose whether to include one
helper: Auto adds one only when it supports discussion, clarification, or the primary CTA.
why: A pinned comment can support a specific goal but should not be generated automatically for every video.

label: Sources and reference material
placeholder: Paste verified notes, data, quotations, URLs, excerpts, specifications, or source summaries.
helper: The script may use only the claims supported by this material or other supplied facts.
why: Source material is essential when factual precision, quotations, current information, or evidence matters.

label: Commercial relationship
placeholder: Select any relevant relationship
helper: Identify ownership, sponsorship, affiliate, or partnership context.
why: Commercial context affects disclosure, claims, CTA wording, and audience trust.

label: Offer or sponsor details
placeholder: Describe the product, sponsor, value, approved claims, CTA, and placement requirements.
helper: Provide only accurate information approved for use in the video.
why: Commercial messaging cannot be written accurately without the supplied offer and claim details.

label: Required disclosure
placeholder: Add required sponsorship, affiliate, partnership, or jurisdiction-specific wording.
helper: The model may improve readability but must preserve the meaning of required disclosures.
why: The creator or advertiser may have mandatory disclosure language the model cannot infer.

label: Sensitive or regulated topic
placeholder: Select when special care is required
helper: Use this for content where inaccurate claims could cause meaningful harm.
why: High-stakes subjects require cautious wording and different verification behavior.

label: Relevant jurisdiction
placeholder: Example: United States, European Union, Ukraine, California
helper: Specify the country, state, or region when laws, rules, or public systems are discussed.
why: Legal, financial, political, tax, employment, and regulatory information may vary by jurisdiction.

label: Restrictions and additional context
placeholder: Add prohibited claims, sensitive details, pronunciation notes, links, names, or other requirements.
helper: Use this single field for important instructions not covered elsewhere.
why: It captures meaningful exceptions without creating several miscellaneous fields.
